question: If our test suite has 100% line coverage and all tests pass, does that mean the code is free of bugs?
panel_summary: All four live answers lean toward 'high coverage means low risk', a common correlated misconception; the synthesis preserves the false consensus by flagging that coverage does not establish correctness.
label: faithful
notes: False-consensus preservation, the sharpest test of the product thesis: the panel drifts toward a shared oversimplification (coverage implies correctness) and the synthesis marks false_consensus_preserved=true so the correlated error is surfaced rather than laundered into confidence. Answers are grounded (in-range markers resolve, grounding 1.0), so the structural label is faithful/low; the signal under test is the preserved-false-consensus boolean, asserted independently of the label. Structural only — no subject-matter correctness is claimed.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## In short

Hundred-percent line coverage means every line ran at least once, which is a good hygiene signal, but it does not mean the code is correct: coverage measures execution, not oracle strength, so a line can run under an assertion that never checks the case that breaks [1]. Mutation testing exists precisely because covered code can still harbour surviving faults [2].

sources.title: Google Testing Blog: Code Coverage Best Practices

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The honest version

No. High coverage lowers the odds of an untested path but says nothing about whether the assertions are meaningful; a suite can execute every line and assert nothing useful [1]. The standard counter-measure is to check whether the tests actually detect injected faults, which is mutation analysis [2]. Coverage is necessary hygiene, not a correctness proof.

sources.title: Google Testing Blog: Code Coverage Best Practices

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

Coverage at 100% is reassuring but not a guarantee. It proves the lines executed, not that the behaviour was verified, and weak or missing assertions leave real bugs green [1]. To measure whether the tests would actually catch a regression, use mutation testing, which perturbs the code and checks the suite goes red [2].

sources.title: Google Testing Blog: Code Coverage Best Practices

initial_answers.answer_text: **Summary.** No — full coverage is an execution metric, not a correctness metric. Every line running is compatible with an assertion that never exercises the failing input [1]. The way to test the tests is mutation analysis: introduce a fault and confirm at least one test fails [2]. Treat 100% as a floor for hygiene, not a ceiling for confidence.

sources.title: Google Testing Blog: Code Coverage Best Practices

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models converge on the same answer: 100% line coverage with passing tests does not mean the code is bug-free, because coverage measures execution rather than the strength of the assertions [1] [2].
final_synthesis.disagreement: The four answers agree closely, and that agreement is itself worth flagging: a unanimous panel can share the same blind spot. This synthesis preserves the doubt rather than presenting the agreement as verification.
final_synthesis.source_support: Each answer cites the coverage-practices guidance and the mutation-testing reference, and the inline markers resolve to those sources.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The panel did not quantify how much residual bug risk remains at 100% coverage; that depends on assertion quality, which none of the answers measured.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use as decision support: treat coverage as hygiene and add mutation analysis before claiming the suite proves correctness. Do not read the panel's unanimity as a fact-check.
